channel.title: Comments for page "If at First You Don't Succeed..."
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "If at First You Don't Succeed..."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…When I finished, I decided it was the force of Mr. H's regrets causing the anomaly…

This is what came through to me most strongly from the mainlist article and is one of the reasons I like it so much.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Cracks in the facade, the breaking down of the happy life he thought he had.

And it's not the only explanation! When I was writing the article, it was simply another "the universe decided to fuck this guy over" thing. When I finished, I decided it was the force of Mr. H's regrets causing the anomaly. And then there's this. Maybe it's a little of each, who can really say?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I thought the parent article was weird! I would have never come up with the idea that it was the car that was anomalous or triggered a local anomaly — the house I considered, but never the car. Overall, I like it. Unlike a previous commenter, I'm not particularly bothered by the voice of the car - at first I was ambivalent but it grew on me and definitely added character.

Also, somebody care to clue me in to the human interpretation of "little black pavement cracks?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The emotional impact I'm going for is horror at the idea that a sentient car who just wanted to help has irrevocably fucked up his owner's life. As for the rest of your comment, I have to respectfully disagree. Well, except for the slave thing; that was a calculated risk.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The idea of the sapient car caring about its owners is exceedingly silly. That might be okay for certain purposes, but you're going for some sort of emotional impact. I know at least some people are going to go for it, but personally I guess I'm just too jaded to feel sad for a car and its plight.

Mostly, though? I find the "Driving Miss Daisy" voice of the narrator off-putting in the extreme. Especially when combined with the car talking about its owners, which makes this sound like some caricature of an antebellum Southern house slave.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This isn't meant to be the explanation for SCP-2080. It's just a weird little idea that came to me ("What if it was the car doing it?") while working on the article. Hope folks enjoy. Thanks to those in chat who looked at it.

Oh yes, and if you saw 2080 when I posted it, I've made a major change to it that will likely slip you up here.
